name: Accessibility Testing
description:
Description:

The web and IT applications plays an essential role in our daily lives. Therefore ensuring that digital products are accessible to all users, including those with disabilities, is necessary. Disabilities can be temporary like a broken arm or more permanent. There are many categories of disabilities such as visual, auditory, motor, cognitive impairments and combinations of multiple disabilities. Attention to accessibility should be taken into account from the start of your development and testing should at least assess compliance with established accessibility standards, such as the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG). Accessibility testing can be seen as a specialism just like security testing. Automated accessibility testing tools can help identify common issues, but can not fully substitute for both expert reviews and user testing with people with disabilities.

Examples of tools include:

  • Design tools like various Design kits for Figma and Sketch like IBM Equal Access
  • Development support like various ESLint plugins, Axe DevTools linter and Axe-core
  • Browser plugins like Taba11y, HeadingsMap, Axe DevTools plugin, IBM Equal Access, WCAG Colour contrast checker and Silktide
  • Automated tools like axe-core, IBM Equal Access and Guidepup
  • Assistive technology like screenreaders such as VoiceOver, NVDA and JAWS, but also platform / OS specific settings

Pros:

  • Early feedback: Many tools can be integrated into your development workflow, providing immediate feedback to designers, developers and testers during the development process.
  • Better for everybody: An accessible application benefits also users without disabilities, the so called 'curb cut effect'.
  • Legal compliance: Accessibility testing helps ensure compliance with legal requirements and standards, reducing the risk of lawsuits and penalties.
  • Broader audience: A significant portion of the population has some form of disability. By making your application accessible, you can reach a wider audience and potentially increase turnover.

Cons:

  • Effort in existing applications: Existing applications may require significant effort to make accessible, especially if accessibility was not considered in earlier development.
  • Knowledge & expertise: Making accessible applications requires specific knowledge and expertise to understand how certain design and development choices impact accessibility. This may require training or consulting specialists.

Conclusion:

Accessibility testing is an essential aspect of modern web development. When taken into consideration from the start, it does not have to take more time or effort than other quality attributes. There are many tools available to facilitate accessible design, development and testing. The impact on users with disabilities should not be underestimated as well as the proportion of people who currently or in the future will have some sort of disability. Accessibility should be a mindset and given the available tooling, there is no reason to at least include a part in your development and testing processes. However, it can take time and effort to make existing applications accessible and may be a learning curve. Only a part can be tested with automated tools, so (periodic) expert reviews and user testing with people with disabilities are essential for your manual testing efforts as well.

name: Agentic Test Writing
description:
Description:

Agentic test writing is an approach to generating automated tests by guiding an AI agent through defined phases: understand the requirements, research the codebase, plan the test, implement it, then validate the result. Each phase builds on the previous one, and the agent stays on track because you have told it exactly what to check and when.
Tools like Claude Code support this through agent skills and CLAUDE.md files, which give the agent project-specific knowledge and a repeatable workflow to follow.

Pros:

  • Structure eliminates the most common mistakes: Wrong locators, made-up translation keys, incorrect accessible roles. These are not random errors. They are predictable. A research phase that explicitly checks components, translation files, and API calls removes them before the agent writes a single line of test code.
  • The workflow improves over time: When the agent makes the same mistake twice, fix it in the skill or context file. The next run, it will not make that mistake again. The setup gets smarter the more you use it, without repeating yourself in every session.
  • Self-validation catches failures before you do: A proper validation phase has the agent run the test, repeat it to check for flakiness, and verify it against your linting rules. You only see a test when it already works.

Cons:

  • Out of the box is a starting point, not a finish line: There are reusable skills and agent instructions available on GitHub that work across different projects. They are a solid place to start. But the real gains come when you tune them to your codebase: your locator strategy, your state setup, your conventions. The closer the skill matches your project, the better the results.
  • It works best in a well-structured project: Strict linting rules, clear component boundaries, consistent naming. The more ambiguous the codebase, the harder it is to give the agent reliable instructions. Cleaning up the project often has to come first.

Conclusion:

Agentic test writing is not a shortcut. It is a discipline. The teams getting real value from it are the ones who invested in writing good agent instructions, defined a clear skill, and treated the validation phase as non-negotiable. The teams who skipped that work got vibe coding results.
The effort pays off. Once a solid skill exists for your project, writing a new E2E test becomes a matter of describing what the feature does and letting the agent handle the rest. That is a meaningful shift in how test automation works.
We recommend adopting this approach if you are already working with a coding agent like Claude Code and Playwright. Start with one type of test, get the skill working reliably, then scale from there.

name: BDD
description:
Description:

BDD (Behavior-Driven Development) is a technique intended to prove that the desired behavior of the application works as part of the development cycle. Acceptance criteria are written as a test to be automated. This makes it one of the easier test-first approaches.
The technique is mostly used to enable easier collaboration between developers, testers, and (business) users. Applying BDD focuses on creating testable user stories that require a lower cognitive load from the developer.
Most popular automation frameworks support a BDD workflow. Tools that explicitly focus on BDD include:

  • Cucumber / Reqnroll (successor of SpecFlow).
  • Behave / JBehave.

Pros:

  • Living documentation: BDD allows spec files to serve as both living documented requirements and tests.
  • Clearer user stories: BDD drives user stories to be clearly defined through testable acceptance criteria.
  • Reduced cognitive load: BDD focuses on reducing functional complexity for developers by focusing on behavior.

Cons:

  • Challenging to implement: Doing BDD correctly is difficult and requires strong collaboration between developers, testers, and business stakeholders, which can be challenging to establish and maintain.
  • Often misused: BDD is frequently misused as merely a way to write tests in a human-readable language. Without the collaborative focus on shared understanding and behavior specification, it holds little value.
  • Misinterpreted as low-code: In some projects, BDD is simplified to using Cucumber and misinterpreted as a low-code automation solution, appealing to teams as a shortcut to start automating. However, BDD's purpose is not to simplify automation but to foster collaboration and ensure a shared understanding of the system's expected behaviors.

Conclusion:

While BDD is widely adopted, it is rarely done right.
In our experience, BDD is rarely applied for its intended purpose, facilitating collaboration and ensuring shared understanding between developers, testers, and business stakeholders. Instead, it often becomes an additional layer of complexity, where the Gherkin syntax adds little value beyond traditional testing approaches.
Where BDD is done right, we see user stories delivered to production faster, and a reduced need for other acceptance level tests.
We recommend BDD when it is supported by business stakeholders, developers, and testers. In other circumstances, it is a wasted effort.

name: Component testing
description:
Description:

Component testing focuses on verifying the functionality of individual components or modules in isolation, independent of the overall system. This approach is particularly effective in modern, modular development practices, such as those using frameworks like React, Angular, or Vue. By isolating components, developers can ensure that each part behaves as expected, enabling faster and more reliable integration into larger systems.

Pros:

  • Proven Effectiveness: Ensures each component functions correctly, reducing bugs during integration.
  • Fast Feedback Loop: Quickly detects issues, enabling rapid fixes during development.
  • Improved Debugging: Failures are scoped to the individual component, simplifying root-cause analysis.

Cons:

  • Requires Complementary Testing: Must be supplemented with integration and end-to-end tests to ensure system-wide functionality.
  • Initial Setup Overhead: Requires tools and mocks for setup, though this effort pays off in the long term.
  • Limited Context: Testing in isolation might miss issues caused by component interactions.

Conclusion:

Component testing is a proven practice that delivers significant value, especially in projects using modular architectures. However, it should complement, not replace, unit testing (UT). Each layer of testing has its own purpose, and overlapping coverage between unit and component tests should be avoided to prevent redundancy.
Additionally, component testing can be effectively combined with visual testing to verify both behavior and UI consistency. We strongly recommend adopting component testing for immediate use wherever it fits project requirements, as part of a comprehensive and efficient testing strategy.

name: Contract Testing
description:
Description:

Contract testing is a methodology for ensuring that two separate systems (such as two microservices) are compatible and can communicate with one another. It captures the interactions that are exchanged between each service, storing them in a contract, which can then be used to verify that both parties adhere to it. Contract testing goes beyond schema testing, requiring both parties to come to a consensus on the allowed set of interactions and allowing for evolution over time.
What sets this form of testing apart from other approaches that aim to achieve the same goal is that each system can be tested independently from the other, and the contract is generated by the code itself, meaning the contract is always kept up to date.

One of the tools most commonly used for contract testing is Pactflow.

Pros:

  • Fast: Contract tests run quickly, are not reliant on other systems, and are easy to maintain.

Cons:

  • Sharing: Contracts must be shared between the producer and the consumer.

Conclusion:

Contract testing is a great addition to any project that deals with large-scale and highly distributed applications. Integration issues between consumers and providers can be detected early without the need for setting up expensive integration environments. We definitely recommend assessing contract testing as a technique.

name: Functional Helpers
description:
Description:

Functional helpers are a lightweight approach to interacting with web page elements in automated tests. Rather than abstracting interactions through page object classes, functional helpers use standalone functions to perform actions directly on the page (e.g., clicking buttons, filling out forms). This approach capitalizes on the composability of modern testing frameworks like Playwright and Cypress, where you call functions that directly interact with elements.
While this approach offers a simpler structure compared to Page Object Models (POM), it can quickly lead to tests that lack clear structure and organization, especially in larger applications. Without careful management, functional helpers can lead to a lack of separation of concerns, where the test logic becomes tightly coupled with how the page is interacted with, making tests harder to read and maintain.

Pros:

  • Simplicity: Functional helpers focus on straightforward, easy-to-understand functions that interact directly with elements, reducing the need for complex abstractions like base page classes.
  • Better debugging: Since functional helpers are simple and typically self-contained, they are easier to debug. Test failures can be traced directly to the helper function without needing to navigate through multiple layers of abstraction.
  • Flexibility and reusability: Tests become more flexible because functional helpers allow easy composition and reuse of actions across different scenarios.

Cons:

  • Harder to read and maintain: One of the key challenges with functional helpers is that they often lack clear separation of concerns. In large tests, this can make it difficult to distinguish between what you're testing (business logic) and how you're interacting with the page (UI logic). This overlap can make tests harder to read, especially when tests grow in complexity.
  • Lack of structure and organization: In larger applications, functional helpers might lead to disorganized code. While functional helpers can be effective for small, isolated tests, they may become harder to manage as tests scale. This can result in repetition, loss of context, and difficulty understanding test scenarios.
  • Potential for code duplication: Without a clear abstraction layer like POM, it's easy for helper functions to be duplicated across tests. Rewriting the same interactions for different tests can increase maintenance overhead, as changes to these actions must be made across multiple places in the codebase.

Conclusion:

Functional helpers may be an appealing choice for applications that heavily rely on UI components, where their lightweight and flexible nature can improve readability and speed of development. In larger applications or where tests become more complex, the lack of clear structure and separation of concerns can lead to maintainability challenges. The coupling of test logic and interaction logic might make tests more difficult to read, especially when working with large teams or scaling the test suite. In these cases, more structured approaches, like POM, combined with composition, may be a better fit to keep tests readable and maintainable in the long term.
Both POM and functional helpers can be excellent choices for your automation framework.

name: Mutation testing
description:
Description:

Conceptually, mutation testing is straightforward. Using specialized tools, small changes (mutations) are made to the source code of an application, creating a "mutant." When tests are executed against this mutant, the expectation is that one or more tests will fail, indicating that the mutation introduced an incorrect system behavior. This process is referred to as "killing the mutant."
If no tests fail, it is a clear indication that the mutated part of the application lacks sufficient test coverage. Mutation testing is often referred to as "testing the tests."

Here are a few examples of mutation testing tools (not an exhaustive list):

  • PIT (pitest.org).
  • Arcmutate (arcmutate.com).
  • Stryker Mutator (stryker-mutator.io).

Pros:

  • Ensures test quality: Mutation testing helps determine not just test coverage but also test effectiveness, ensuring your tests can detect actual faults in the code.
  • Identifies weak tests: It can highlight weak or redundant tests that might be passing but are not effectively validating the application's behavior.

Cons:

  • High computational cost: Mutation testing can be computationally expensive, especially in large projects, as it requires running the test suite multiple times for each mutation.
  • Additional setup effort: It may require additional configuration or tuning to integrate seamlessly with your existing testing pipelines.

Conclusion:

Mutation testing is an amazing technique to validate if you can actually rely on your tests to inform you of changed behavior. Traditional test coverage (i.e., line, statement, branch, etc.) measures only which code is executed by your tests. It does not check that your tests are actually able to detect faults in the executed code. It is therefore only able to identify code that is definitely not tested. Mutation testing is much better, as it is actually able to detect whether each statement is meaningfully tested, therefore giving an accurate report on the quality of your tests.
We would definitely recommend checking it out!

name: Observability Testing
description:
Description:

Observability (testing) refers to monitoring the internal state of your system by collecting and analyzing telemetry data (such as logs, metrics, and traces) during both testing and production. This provides deep insights into what happens during test execution or real-world usage.
Rather than simply knowing that a test or deployment failed, observability enables you to determine why it failed and where in the system the issue occurred. This supports rapid diagnosis, root cause analysis, and validation of system behavior under real conditions.
While observability has its roots in production monitoring, its application in testing is becoming increasingly relevant in automated testing, especially within distributed systems and CI/CD pipelines. Integrating observability testing early in the software lifecycle (e.g., in test or staging environments) helps teams prepare for full-lifecycle observability. It lays the foundation for practices like progressive delivery (a strategy for gradually rolling out features, often using mechanisms such as feature flags) and helps uncover bugs earlier, pinpoint test flakiness, and even detect problems in the test environment itself.

Examples of common open-source tools and frameworks include:

  • Tracetest: Enables trace-based testing using OpenTelemetry, allowing assertions on spans (individual operations or steps within a distributed trace, such as an API call or database query) and integration with tools like Jaeger and Grafana.
  • Malabi: A trace-based testing library for Node.js applications that captures OpenTelemetry traces automatically during integration tests.
  • Prometheus: A metrics collection and alerting toolkit widely used for monitoring performance.
  • Grafana: A visualization platform often paired with Prometheus for real-time dashboarding.
  • Jaeger/Grafana Tempo: Tools for distributed tracing and analyzing request paths across services.
  • OpenSearch: A log and trace analysis engine built on Elasticsearch, often used with Kibana-style dashboards.
  • SigNoz: A unified observability platform supporting metrics, traces, and logs in one UI.

Pros:

  • Faster diagnosis of failures: By capturing detailed logs and trace information during test execution, teams can quickly identify the root cause of issues without reproducing them manually.
  • Improved test confidence: Helps differentiate between genuine application bugs and environment-related flakiness or instability in the test setup.
  • Insight into trends: Allows teams to monitor long-term trends in test behavior and system reliability, offering data-driven insights for test optimization.
  • Enhanced developer feedback: Developers receive clearer signals from test failures, accelerating the development and debugging cycle.

Cons:

  • Setup complexity: Implementing observability testing requires consistent logging and monitoring infrastructure, which may be difficult to implement into legacy systems.
  • Tooling overhead: Integrating observability tools (e.g., tracing systems, log aggregators, etc.) may require additional resources and operational support.
  • Data volume: If you do not manage the volume of data properly, it can become overwhelming and make analysis harder rather than easier.

Conclusion:

Observability testing is a great enabler for modern test strategies, especially in distributed, asynchronous, or event-driven systems. While often associated with production monitoring, its application during automated testing provides a deeper understanding of failure modes and test stability.
We recommend adopting observability practices in any system where test reliability, fast debugging, or system-level visibility are important. However, teams should carefully consider the infrastructure and the skills required to implement it effectively. When integrated properly, observability testing transforms test automation from a black-box signal into a rich, actionable feedback loop.

name: Page Object Model
description:
Description:

The Page Object Model (POM) is a design pattern commonly used to structure test code, typically reflecting the structure of the application under test. By encapsulating the logic for interacting with a page in a single location, POM reduces code duplication and enhances test maintainability. Typically, these page objects extend a base class that includes shared methods for interacting with common page elements, such as clicking buttons or filling out forms. However, modern test frameworks like Playwright and Cypress offer built-in functionality for interacting with page elements, making the need for base classes less critical.
As frameworks like React have popularized the use of UI components, test frameworks should adopt a similar approach. Instead of relying on base pages, consider using component objects and applying composition to your page objects. This approach ensures that your framework closely mirrors the system under test.

Pros:

  • Reduced code duplication: POM helps centralize the logic for interacting with page elements in a single class. This minimizes code repetition, making it easier to maintain test scripts when the UI changes.
  • Improved test maintainability: Since the page logic is separated into different classes, updating the UI (e.g., changing button names or locations) only requires changes to the corresponding page object class, not throughout every test script.
  • Separation of concerns: POM separates the test logic (what you are testing) from the interaction logic (how you interact with the page), making your tests cleaner, more organized, and easier to read.

Cons:

  • Increased complexity for small projects: For small projects, the overhead of creating and maintaining page objects may outweigh the benefits. In these cases, a simpler approach may be more appropriate.
  • Dependency on UI structure: If the UI changes frequently, maintaining page objects can become time-consuming. In these cases, consider using more flexible selectors or alternative testing strategies.
  • Over-abstraction: Overusing the POM can lead to overly complex test code, making it harder to understand and maintain. Be cautious of creating unnecessary levels of abstraction.

Conclusion:

The POM can be an effective design pattern, especially for large and complex applications. However, it's important to be mindful of the potential drawbacks and strike a careful balance between principles like Don't Repeat Yourself (DRY) and Keep It Simple, Stupid (KISS). By considering these principles, POM can still be a valid and valuable choice for structuring your test code, as long as you apply it thoughtfully.
Alternatively, if your application primarily consists of components, you could also apply the functional helper pattern.

name: Shift Left
description:
Description:

Shift Left involves moving testing activities earlier in the software development lifecycle (SDLC), sometimes even before code is written. The goal is to detect and resolve bugs as early as possible, which reduces cost, accelerates feedback, and improves software quality.

Examples of Shift Left practices:

  • Early testing during analysis/design: Define test scenarios or acceptance criteria (e.g., using BDD/Gherkin) as part of requirements gathering.
  • Unit & integration testing: Developers write tests alongside code, catching bugs earlier and ensuring faster resolution.
  • Test automation: Automate tests (that add business value) to run continuously in CI/CD pipelines.
  • Static analysis & linting: Tools like ESLint, SonarQube, or TypeScript check code quality and catch errors during development.
  • Security Shift Left (DevSecOps): Apply security checks (e.g., dependency scanning, static security analysis) at commit or build time.

Pros:

  • Faster development cycles: Early bug detection reduces rework and shortens delivery timelines.
  • Enhanced collaboration: Developers, testers, and business stakeholders work closely from the start.
  • Increased automation & coverage: Automated tests provide rapid feedback and higher confidence in changes.

Cons:

  • Cultural shift required: Teams need to adopt new mindsets, tools, and workflows.
  • Initial investment: Time, tooling, and training are needed upfront.
  • Incomplete real-world coverage: Some issues may still only appear later on in the process or in production.
  • Over-reliance on pre-release testing: Focusing too much on early-stage checks may lead to thinking about and or missing issues that only surface in real-world usage or production environments.

Conclusion:

We recommend adopting Shift Left practices to detect defects earlier and improve overall software quality. In doing so, we often advise teams to re-evaluate the role of slower, UI-based E2E tests in favor of faster feedback layers like API and unit testing.
In practice, we often see that Shift Left has a significant impact on the overall test (automation) strategy, requiring thoughtful integration ideally in combination with Shift Right practices to ensure quality throughout the entire software lifecycle.

name: Shift Right
description:
Description:

Shift Right focuses on testing, validation, and monitoring after software has been released into production or near-production environments. It complements pre-release testing by using real-world feedback, usage patterns, and system behavior to ensure reliability and continuous improvement.

Observability Testing (using logs, metrics, and traces) is one example of Shift Right, but Shift Right also includes techniques like:

  • Canary releases: Gradually releasing features to a subset of users and monitoring their impact.
  • Chaos testing: Introducing controlled failures to test system resilience (e.g., with tools like Chaos Monkey).
  • Feature toggles & A/B testing: Controlling feature rollout and gathering user feedback before full deployment.
  • Service monitoring & alerting: Using dashboards, logs, and tracing to detect and respond to issues in real time.

Pros:

  • Real-world validation: Ensures the software works as expected in actual usage conditions.
  • Continuous improvement: Live metrics and user feedback drive ongoing enhancements.
  • Improved user experience: Allows rapid detection and resolution of issues affecting users.

Cons:

  • Risk of user impact: Bugs in production may affect real users before they are fixed.
  • Requires operational maturity: Without mature CI/CD pipelines and alerting, the benefits of Shift Right can be limited or delayed.
  • Dependent on usage data: Low user activity can limit the effectiveness of Shift Right strategies.

Conclusion:

We recommend adopting Shift Right practices to validate software in real-world conditions and drive continuous improvement after release. In our experience, teams that invest in observability, feature toggles, and experimentation gain critical insights that traditional testing cannot provide. Failures in production, when properly monitored, offer valuable input for improving both resilience and user experience. Shift Right requires a certain level of operational maturity to be effective. We advise combining it with Shift Left practices to create a balanced quality strategy across the full software lifecycle.

name: TDD
description:
Description:

TDD, or Test-Driven Development, is a practice used in software development to create high-quality, neat, and error-free code, especially when change is likely to happen later on. As the name suggests, TDD is a development approach driven by tests rather than first writing your code and adding unit tests afterward.

Pros:

  • Test coverage: By writing tests before you code, you know all your written code is covered by unit tests.
  • Quality: The quality of your code will improve because you need to think about scenarios and functionality before you write a single line of code.

Cons:

  • Learning curve: It can be difficult to start with TDD; it takes time and effort to invest in this technique.

Conclusion:

TDD can dramatically improve the quality of your code as it forces you to think about tests before writing code and gives you rapid feedback when making changes.
We recommend evaluating if TDD fits within the context of your organization, e.g., if your product is relatively well-defined and doesn't change in extreme forms very rapidly.

name: Vibe Coding
description:
Description:

Vibe coding is an innovative approach that leverages artificial intelligence and natural language processing to write and generate code. Instead of manually coding, developers and QA engineers utilize AI-powered tools to translate written natural language instructions into functional code snippets, functions, or even entire applications.
This approach transforms the traditional coding process, making it more accessible, faster (potentially), and aligned with human language, reducing barriers for non-experts and increasing productivity for experienced developers.

Pros:

  • Accelerated development: Significantly reduces the time required to write boilerplate code or implement features.
  • Faster refactoring: Refactor existing codebases more efficiently by using AI-generated suggestions.
  • Lower barrier to entry: Empowers non-developers or less experienced team members to contribute through natural language prompts.

Cons:

  • AI is not always accurate: Generated code may require review, multiple iterations, and adjustments to be correct, especially in larger existing codebases.
  • Potential for over-reliance: Developers may become overly dependent on AI tools, leading to a decline in traditional coding skills and problem-solving abilities.
  • Potential for technical debt, security, and code quality issues: The generated code might work initially but can be inefficient, difficult to understand, or hard to maintain in the long run, especially for complex systems. Without careful human review and refinement, it can lead to spaghetti code and future headaches. AI might also overlook critical security measures, such as proper input validation or error handling. Relying solely on AI without thorough security reviews can introduce vulnerabilities into applications, leading to potential hacks or data breaches. This often stems from missing the correct context, which is not provided.
  • Potential for ambiguity: Natural language instructions can sometimes be vague, leading to unintended code outputs.

Conclusion:

Vibe coding could represent a paradigm shift in software development, enabling developers to focus more on high-level design and problem-solving rather than low-level coding tasks. However, it is important to approach this technique with caution; AI-generated code is not always accurate and may require multiple iterations to get right. This is where it becomes difficult; less experienced developers may not be able to determine whether the generated code is correct, leading to potential issues in the codebase.
Simply put, vibe coding can enhance productivity, but a fool with a tool is still a fool. Vibe coding can be a powerful tool to create boilerplate code, refactor existing code, or even write entire applications. However, it should not be seen as a replacement for traditional coding practices, especially in complex systems where understanding the context and nuances of the code is crucial. We recommend assessing the potential of vibe coding in your organization, especially if you have a large codebase or complex systems. It can be a valuable tool for experienced developers, but it should not replace traditional coding practices entirely.

name: Visual Regression Testing
description:
Description:

If your team or company has a static component library, performing visual regression tests can provide significant value by offering visual validation of these components on every iteration. While you could detect these differences manually, sometimes they are extremely small and the process can be time-consuming. Most tools offer a pixel-by-pixel comparison and work similarly. The challenge lies in finding a threshold that strikes the perfect balance between avoiding flaky tests, missing regressions, or constantly updating snapshots.

Examples of popular visual testing tools include:

  • Chromatic
  • Percy
  • Test tool implementations, e.g., Playwright, WebdriverIO
  • Applitools

Pros:

  • Ease of implementation: Many visual testing tools are simple to set up and integrate into existing workflows.
  • Automated visual monitoring: Tools can monitor visual regressions more easily than doing so manually.

Cons:

  • Flakiness: Visual comparisons can result in flaky tests if thresholds are not correctly set, leading to time-consuming debugging tasks.
  • Pricing: High-quality tools are often paid, and performing many comparisons can significantly increase licensing costs.

Conclusion:

Visual regression testing can be very beneficial when implemented correctly, preventing unnoticed UI changes and improving overall design consistency. However, improper setup can turn it into a time sink.
For teams using Storybook, visual testing is an especially natural fit, as it enables seamless validation of UI components in isolation. If your project includes a well-defined, static component library, investing in visual regression testing is highly recommended. Otherwise, teams may find better value in other QA activities.

name: AI Assistants (such as Github Co-pilot and JetBrains AI Assistant)
description:
Description:

AI Assistants are transforming the development landscape by acting as intelligent helpers, capable of providing real-time code suggestions, generating boilerplate code, and even acting as a rubber duck for debugging. By leveraging large language models trained on vast amounts of code, these tools can significantly speed up development workflows and reduce repetitive tasks.
However, they come with notable challenges, particularly in areas like code quality, privacy, and potential over-reliance.

Pros:

  • Increased productivity: AI Assistants help developers work faster with intelligent suggestions, auto-completion, and context-aware code explanations.
  • Wide language support: Due to training on existing material, AI Assistants support a wide range of languages and can provide relevant suggestions.
  • Boilerplate generation: AI Assistants quickly generate boilerplate code to help developers get started with new projects or focus on more complex tasks.

Cons:

  • Dependency risk: Over-reliance on AI Assistants may lead to reduced problem-solving skills and overdependence on automated suggestions among developers, especially beginners.
  • Code quality: AI Assistants still make mistakes, and the code they generate should never be blindly accepted without verifying the output.
  • Privacy: AI Assistants are trained on public repositories and will use your code to improve their models without enterprise subscriptions. Enterprise solutions that do not use your code to improve their models and that filter responses based on license types exist but are costly.

Conclusion:

AI Assistants are powerful tools that can significantly boost productivity, streamline development, and reduce repetitive tasks. However, they require careful adoption due to risks like dependency, code quality issues, and privacy concerns. OpenAI and similar companies have faced legal challenges over the use of copyrighted material for training models, underscoring the need for caution. For organizations or developers who can mitigate these risks, whether through enterprise-level tools or strict validation processes, AI Assistants can be a valuable addition to the modern development toolkit.
We recommend exploring AI Assistants to enhance workflows, especially in repetitive or boilerplate-heavy tasks, while keeping risks in check.

name: Axe-core
description:
Description:

Axe-core is an open-source accessibility testing engine developed by Deque Systems. This engine is the underlying technology for many other accessibility testing tools. This includes plugins for browser testing like @axe-core/playwright, @axe-core/webdriverio and cypress-axe. However, also jest-axe for unittesting, Storybook accessibility in the design phase and Chrome Lighthouse are based on Axe-core.
Axe-core scans HTML and reports issues found regarding web accessibility based on various standards and guidelines, including WCAG. In addition, it reports potential issues that need manual validation. It can be used in various phases of development like component testing, automated E2E testing and manual testing.

Pros:

  • Proven technology: Issues reported by axe-core are reliable and the ruleset is maintained by a team of accessibility experts.
  • Versatile: Axe-core can be integrated into various development and testing workflows, including CI/CD pipelines.
  • Documentation: Axe-core has extensive documentation on accessibility violations, the various rulesets and implementation suggestions.

Cons:

  • Extensive data: There is a large amount of raw data reported when used as a library. For reporting purposes it may require extra work.
  • Configuration and use: Axe-core is very configurable, but this can lead to complexity when setting it up and taking it into use, resulting in a possible false sense of conformity. Configuring an incomplete set of tags, missing or testing incorrect states and not following up on findings that require manual validation can lead to accessibility issues being overlooked.
  • Overreliance: Knowledge about accessibility is still required to effectively use axe-core. Some issues require manual validation or can not be found by automated tests.

Conclusion:

Axe-core has proven to be a reliable tool for automated accessibility testing. It comes in the forms of a library, linter and browser plugin and is also the underlying technology for many other accessibility testing tools. Its versatility and proven ruleset make it a solid choice for integrating accessibility testing into development and testing workflows.
However, users should be aware of the complexity involved in configuring and using axe-core effectively. Also overreliance on automated testing should be avoided, as some issues require manual validation or can not be found by automated tests.

name: Bruno
description:
Description:

Bruno is an open-source tool for sending and inspecting API requests and responses. It supports common API styles such as REST and GraphQL and is completely free to use, with no account or subscription required. Unlike many modern API tools, Bruno runs entirely locally and does not rely on cloud services. Its minimal and straightforward interface makes it easy to send API requests quickly and work with API collections across multiple environments.

Pros:

  • Local-first and privacy-friendly: Bruno runs fully locally and does not sync data to the cloud. This makes it suitable for security-sensitive projects where data must stay within the developer’s environment.
  • Open source and free: Bruno is completely free to use and does not require an account, paid plan, or monthly credits.
  • Git-friendly: API collections are stored as plain text files, which integrates naturally with Git workflows and allows easy sharing, reviewing, and versioning.

Cons:

  • Limited workflows: Bruno only supports basic pre-request and post-response scripts and does not offer advanced workflows or automation features.
  • Smaller community and ecosystem As a relatively new tool, Bruno currently has a smaller community, fewer tutorials, integrations, and third-party plugins compared to more established tools.
  • No built-in cloud collaboration : There is no cloud sync or real-time shared workspace. Team collaboration relies entirely on Git and external tooling.

Conclusion:

Bruno is a simple and efficient tool that does exactly what it is designed for: quickly sending and receiving API requests. Its philosophy and feature set are reminiscent of the early versions of Postman, before cloud-centric platforms became the norm.
It is an excellent choice for developers who prefer a lightweight, local, and Git-based workflow. For teams that require advanced collaboration features, automation, or cloud-based tooling, Bruno may feel limited. However, for fast and straightforward API testing, it offers a clean and developer-friendly experience.
In short, Bruno is a serious and capable alternative to Postman — especially who value simplicity, speed, and full control over their workflow. It is definitely worth trying.

name: Chromatic
description:
Description:

Chromatic is a SaaS platform that simplifies Visual Regression Testing by automatically generating visual snapshots for every story in your Storybook. It provides an intuitive interface for detecting and managing changes, making it easy for developers to identify visual regressions and approve updates.
Chromatic supports seamless integration with Storybook, and its free tier makes it accessible for smaller projects or initial trials.

Pros:

  • Automatic tests: Chromatic automatically generates tests for each individual story with Storybook.
  • Easy snapshot updates: Updating snapshots in Chromatic is extremely easy and does not require jumping through hoops with Docker containers that might be necessary with other tools for reliable snapshot comparison.
  • Simple integration: The integration with Storybook is straightforward and requires minimal configuration to get started.

Cons:

  • Subscription required: Chromatic requires a subscription to unlock all features and browsers.
  • Storybook dependency: Chromatic requires Storybook to work.

Conclusion:

For teams already using Storybook, adopting Chromatic is a natural choice. It provides instant visual regression testing capabilities with minimal effort, allowing developers to detect and manage visual changes efficiently. Its free tier is ideal for smaller teams or initial experiments, and its subscription unlocks advanced features for larger-scale projects.
If you're looking for an easy way to incorporate visual regression testing into your workflow, Chromatic is definitely worth exploring.

name: Claude Code
description:
Description:

Claude Code is Anthropic's agentic coding tool that runs directly in your terminal. Unlike AI assistants that suggest code inline, Claude Code works autonomously: it reads your codebase, plans a solution, writes code, runs commands, and validates its own output. It operates on full context, not just the file you have open.
In test automation, Claude Code can research your application, identify locators, understand your testing conventions, and write complete test suites with minimal back and forth. It supports project-specific instructions through CLAUDE.md files and reusable workflows through agent skills.

Pros:

  • Truly autonomous: Claude Code does not just suggest code. It plans, researches, implements, and validates. This makes it genuinely useful for multi-step tasks like writing a full E2E test from scratch.
  • Full codebase context: Claude Code reads actual files in your project, which means it can find existing patterns, reuse helpers, and align with your conventions rather than making things up.
  • Customizable with instructions and skills: CLAUDE.md files give the agent project-specific knowledge. Agent skills let you define repeatable workflows, for example a structured process for writing and validating Playwright tests.

Cons:

  • Token cost: Claude Code works with large context windows and runs multiple steps per task. On larger codebases or complex tasks, this can add up quickly.
  • Requires good setup to work well: Without clear agent instructions and project context, Claude Code can still make the same mistakes as unstructured vibe coding. The quality of your instructions determines the quality of the output.

Conclusion:

Claude Code is not a better autocomplete. It is a different way of working entirely. For test automation, it can take care of the heavy lifting: researching the application, identifying the right locators, setting up test state, and running the tests to verify they actually work.
That said, giving it clear instructions and guardrails is not optional. A well-structured skill with a proper validation phase will produce reliable tests. Leaving it to figure things out on its own will not.
Claude Code has proven itself widely across the industry. We recommend adopting it, especially for teams already working with Playwright or other modern test frameworks where the context is well-defined.
To get the most out of it, invest in agent skills and proper project instructions. Without those, you are leaving most of the value on the table. If you are new to working with Claude Code and want to learn how to set it up effectively, Anthropic offers learning resources at anthropic.com/learn.

name: K6
description:
Description:

K6 is an open-source performance testing tool designed for modern developers and DevOps teams. Built with a focus on load testing, K6 enables users to write tests in both JavaScript and TypeScript and execute them efficiently at scale. This makes K6 particularly suitable for an audience with a development background. It is especially well-suited for testing APIs, microservices, and web applications, offering smooth integration with CI/CD pipelines. K6 is scriptable, lightweight, and can be run locally or in the cloud.
K6 provides a user interface with Grafana K6 Studio. It is free to use, with an active community contributing to improvements and new features.

Pros:

  • Developer-friendly: K6 is written in JavaScript and TypeScript, making it easy for developers to script and maintain tests.
  • Distributed testing: K6 allows for distributed testing, enabling users to run tests across multiple machines and simulate real-world scenarios, making it possible to test the scalability of your application.
  • Detailed metrics: K6 provides detailed insights into response times, request rates, and system behavior under load.

Cons:

  • Grafana Cloud K6: Grafana Cloud K6 is a paid service that offers additional features like cloud execution and advanced analytics, meaning that additional possibilities require a paid plan.
  • Not language agnostic: K6 is limited to JavaScript and TypeScript, which may not be ideal for teams using or familiar with other languages.
  • Learning curve: While K6 is developer-friendly, users might face a learning curve if they are not familiar with performance testing.

Conclusion:

K6 is a powerful and efficient tool for load testing, particularly for teams looking to integrate performance testing into their CI/CD pipelines.
While it lacks a detailed and mature UI and built-in functional testing features, its scripting capabilities and high performance make it an excellent choice for developers focused on API and application performance.

name: Playwright CLI
description:
Description:

Playwright CLI (@playwright/cli) is a command-line tool built specifically for AI coding agents. Instead of streaming browser state into the model's context window like MCP does, it saves snapshots to disk as YAML files. Agents issue shell commands like open, click, and snapshot, and read results from disk on demand. Browsing a flow produces reusable Playwright test code as output, making it a natural companion to Claude Code and the agentic test writing approach.

Pros:

  • Lower token usage than MCP: Browser state stays on disk, not in the context window. This directly affects cost and how long an agent can work before hitting context limits.
  • Clean context throughout the session: Each snapshot overwrites the previous one. No stale page data accumulates, so decision quality stays consistent from start to finish.
  • Works with any coding agent out of the box: Because playwright-cli is just shell commands, any agent that can run a terminal (Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Windsurf) can use it immediately with no MCP client configuration or protocol integration required.

Cons:

  • Requires filesystem and shell access: Does not work in sandboxed environments. A natural fit for Claude Code, but incompatible with some setups.
  • Not plug-and-play with chat clients: No direct integration with Claude Desktop or VS Code Copilot. It requires a coding agent with filesystem access.

Conclusion:

If you are still using Playwright MCP, now is a good time to switch. Playwright CLI gives you the same browser control with a fraction of the token cost and none of the context pollution. We recommend starting with Playwright CLI over MCP for any new AI-driven test automation work, especially if you are moving towards a more agentic testing approach.

name: Playwright MCP
description:
Description:

Playwright MCP is Microsoft's Model Context Protocol server for browser automation. It exposes Playwright's browser control as callable functions for AI models, streaming full context back into the conversation on every interaction: accessibility trees, console output, and screenshots. It integrates directly with MCP-compatible clients like Claude Desktop and VS Code Copilot with no filesystem setup required.

Pros:

  • Zero friction to get started: No filesystem dependency. If your tool supports MCP, you can connect it to a browser in minutes.
  • Works in sandboxed environments: No shell or filesystem access needed, making it the right choice when agent isolation is a hard requirement.

Cons:

  • High token consumption: Full accessibility trees stream into the context window on every interaction. A single page snapshot can cost 800 or more tokens, and it adds up fast.
  • Context pollution over time: Stale page state accumulates in the conversation. Agents start reasoning from earlier states rather than the current one, leading to fabricated selectors and incorrect actions.

Conclusion:

We put Playwright MCP on Hold. If you are already using it, it is not broken, but we recommend moving to Playwright CLI. The token savings, cleaner context, and better fit with coding agent workflows make it the stronger choice for any serious test automation work.

name: Postman
description:
Description:

Postman is a tool that allows users to interact with APIs and supports widely used variants such as REST, GraphQL, and SOAP. Its easy-to-use interface allows just about anyone to perform tests quickly. Using the Newman CLI, Postman offers integration with CI/CD pipelines. It also allows for writing tests in JavaScript directly in Postman.

Pros:

  • User friendly: Regardless of technical skills, most testers should find their way in Postman very quickly.
  • Request history: Postman maintains a history of requests, which is especially useful when testing an API and can make the reproduction of potential issues easier.
  • Environment variables: Postman allows you to easily set and change environment variables.

Cons:

  • Code-oriented preference: Developers usually prefer more code-oriented tools. In most cases, it makes sense to store API tests as close to the API source as possible and use the same language that the API is built in, which is usually not JavaScript.
  • Performance: Especially when dealing with very large APIs and datasets, the Postman UI can become less responsive.
  • Paid plan: Postman is not free for enterprises, and there are many other very good free alternatives available.

Conclusion:

Postman is a good choice for testers who need an easy-to-use interface for API testing, especially for quick manual validations.
For automation, however, more scalable and development-friendly alternatives are available, so we do not recommend using Postman for new automation projects. If you are already using it, there is no immediate need to migrate.

name: Storybook
description:
Description:

Storybook is a front-end workshop for building UI components and pages in isolation. Storybook can be used with React, Vue, Angular, Web Components, and more.
Storybook serves as a library of components that can be shared and interacted with by developers, designers, and other stakeholders, making it a valuable tool for collaboration and reusability.

Pros:

  • Develop in isolation: Storybook allows components to be developed and tested independently without needing to spin up an entire app.
  • Uniform apps: Storybook makes it easier to create a large number of apps with a uniform look and feel and reduces the effort in implementing reusable components.
  • Testability: Standardized UI components in Storybook make testing (e.g., unit, integration, accessibility and visual) more efficient and reliable.

Cons:

  • Expensive: Creating a UI library with Storybook is expensive and likely not worth it for smaller projects.
  • Steep learning curve: Initial setup tends to be challenging. Storybook has a steep learning curve when maximizing its use.

Conclusion:

Storybook is an excellent tool for large-scale projects or organizations with multiple applications, offering reusability, collaboration, and consistency. It aligns well with component testing principles and supports visual testing to validate both behavior and appearance. Additionally, it includes a Playwright-based Test Runner that enables component, UI, and visual testing directly within the Storybook environment.
While the setup cost and learning curve make it less ideal for smaller projects, we recommend it for teams aiming to scale and maintain high-quality, uniform UI systems.

name: Testcontainers
description:
Description:

Testcontainers attempt to solve complex environment configurations by providing ready-made Docker containers for commonly used databases, message brokers, and/or browsers. It allows you to define these dependencies as code, making them easier to maintain. It supports a wide range of tooling out of the box.

Pros:

  • Rich tool support: Testcontainers offer over 50 commonly used databases, message brokers, and other tools out of the box.
  • Productivity: Increases productivity by offloading the complexity of managing the test environment to Testcontainers.
  • Programming language support: Testcontainers support just about any programming language.

Cons:

  • Black box: It can be slightly challenging to debug the black box nature of Testcontainers.
  • Resource intensive: Running multiple containers simultaneously can be resource-heavy, potentially impacting system performance during test runs.

Conclusion:

In most cases, running tests is complicated due to having many dependencies, such as a database and/or message broker, or sometimes even both. Testcontainers come with almost all industry-standard tools pre-packaged in a container and include orchestration logic. This significantly reduces the effort required to set up and maintain test environments while improving reliability and reproducibility.
While debugging and resource use can pose challenges, the benefits far outweigh the drawbacks. We highly recommend adopting Testcontainers to streamline your testing workflow and simplify dependency management.

name: Applitools
description:
Description:

Applitools is a visual testing platform that leverages AI-powered algorithms to perform visual regression testing. It focuses exclusively on visual testing and requires integration with a cloud vendor or local grid for test execution, as it does not include a proper execution environment. By sending test data to Applitools for analysis, teams can ensure UI consistency across multiple browsers and devices.

Pros:

  • AI-powered accuracy: Reduces false positives by intelligently detecting meaningful visual differences.
  • Broad framework compatibility: Works seamlessly with tools like Selenium, Playwright, and WebdriverIO.
  • Cross-platform testing: Ensures consistent UI experiences across browsers and devices.

Cons:

  • Requires external grid: Teams must pair it with a cloud vendor or local grid for executing tests, as Applitools only handles the visual validation aspect.
  • Data dependency: All test data must be sent to Applitools, which could be a concern for teams with strict data security policies.
  • Expensive for scaling: Costs can escalate quickly for teams with large-scale or enterprise-level visual testing needs.

Conclusion:

Applitools offers an exceptional visual testing solution, backed by AI for accuracy and efficiency. However, its reliance on external execution environments and its pricing structure may pose challenges for some teams. It is best suited for teams that already have access to a test execution grid or cloud vendor and are looking to enhance their visual regression testing capabilities.
We recommend trialing Applitools to determine its value for your workflows, especially if visual consistency is a high priority for your projects.

name: BrowserStack
description:
Description:

BrowserStack is a cloud-based testing platform that has been a trusted name in the industry for nearly as long as Sauce Labs. Unlike its competitors, BrowserStack focuses heavily on developer and small-team workflows, offering extensive integrations and features designed for streamlined testing. However, it does not provide support for emulators and simulators, differentiating it from alternatives like LambdaTest or Sauce Labs.

Pros:

  • Developer-Focused: Tailored for developers and small teams with features and integrations that simplify workflows and testing processes.
  • Stability and Reliability: Provides a stable and consistent experience, particularly for browser, and real device testing.
  • Extensive Browser and Device Coverage: Supports a wide range of browsers and physical devices, ensuring compatibility testing across diverse environments.

Cons:

  • No Emulator/Simulator Support: Lacks virtual emulators and simulators for Android and iOS, which limits flexibility for teams that rely on these for mobile testing.
  • Limited Focus on Enterprise Scaling: While excellent for small teams, it may lack some of the advanced enterprise-level features available in other platforms.

Conclusion:

BrowserStack remains a reliable and trusted option, particularly for developers and smaller teams focused on browser and physical device testing. Its stability, integrations and broad coverage, make it an excellent choice for compatibility testing.
However, the lack of emulator and simulator support limits its utility for teams requiring virtual environments for mobile testing.

name: GitHub Actions
description:
Description:

GitHub Actions is a CI/CD platform integrated into GitHub, enabling automation of workflows triggered by events like commits, PRs, or releases. It offers a developer-focused experience, supporting most enterprise and modern CI/CD needs, backed by a large ecosystem of reusable Actions. It is widely adopted in the open-source community, making it highly familiar to many engineers and lowering onboarding time in enterprise settings.
While GitHub Actions can be used as a general Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) orchestrator, its full value is realized when GitHub is the central DevOps platform. Using it in isolation and other third-party solutions adds complexity and may underutilize its native integrations.

Pros:

  • Automation Support: Strong test automation features with matrix builds, artifacts, and test reports.
  • Open-Source Friendly: Familiar and free for open-source projects, encouraging wider internal adoption.
  • Developer Experience: Extensive GitHub Marketplace and YAML-based workflows.
  • Enterprise Features: Includes SSO, audit logs, IP policies, and self-hosted runners.

Cons:

  • GitHub-Centric: Limited value outside the GitHub ecosystem.
  • Vendor Lock-In: Workflow lock-in and migration effort to other CI/CD tools.
  • Visualization Limitations: No extensive pipeline visualization.
  • Security Concerns: Many Actions are community-maintained or third-party, which may introduce security risks.

Conclusion:

We see GitHub Actions as a powerful platform for test automation and SDLC integration, especially for teams already working with GitHub. Its widespread use in the open-source community makes it a natural and familiar choice within many organizations. We recommend using GitHub Actions when GitHub is fully integrated into your development process.
If that’s not the case, other tools may offer better alignment with your existing toolchain.

name: LambdaTest
description:
Description:

LambdaTest is a cloud-based testing platform that has quickly emerged as a strong contender in the test automation space. Known for its rapid feature development, LambdaTest offers a high-performance infrastructure for virtual browsers, emulators, and simulators. However, its unique implementation of capabilities and differences in defaults compared to tools like Selenium and Appium can make migrations more complex. Its recent introduction of Kane AI, a powerful AI-driven solution, sets it apart from competitors by enhancing test analysis and efficiency.

Pros:

  • Rapid feature development: Continuously rolling out innovative tools and capabilities, providing users with cutting-edge features.
  • Performant emulator/simulator infrastructure: Offers reliable and fast environments for Android and iOS testing.
  • Kane AI: A unique AI solution that enhances workflows, accelerates test analysis, and improves efficiency.

Cons:

  • Migration complexity: Differences in capabilities and defaults compared to tools like Selenium and Appium can complicate transitions from local grids or other platforms.
  • Documentation challenges: Documentation lacks clarity and depth in certain areas, making it harder for users to fully leverage the platform's features.
  • Stability for large test suites: While suitable for smaller projects and organizations, stability concerns with large-scale test suites or enterprise-level testing make it riskier without thorough evaluation.

Conclusion:

LambdaTest is a fast-growing platform with a strong focus on innovation, highlighted by its high-performance infrastructure and Kane AI solution, which offers a significant competitive advantage in test execution, analysis, and efficiency. It is particularly well suited for smaller projects or organizations looking for a feature-rich and modern testing platform.
However, teams considering LambdaTest for larger projects or enterprise use should evaluate it carefully, given migration complexities, documentation gaps, and stability concerns. We recommend trialing LambdaTest to assess its fit, especially for teams aiming to leverage its cutting-edge AI capabilities and scalable infrastructure.

name: Sauce Labs
description:
Description:

Sauce Labs is a cloud-based platform for cross-browser and cross-device testing, offering a virtual browser/(real) device cloud. It has been a trusted solution in the industry for years, providing extensive browser and (real) device coverage with strong documentation and support.

Pros:

  • Established Platform: A reliable name in the industry with a proven track record.
  • Reliable Real Device Features: While it offers fewer features for real devices compared to competitors, the existing features are more stable and reliable, making it a strong option for scaling large test suites.
  • Wide Coverage: Supports a variety of browsers and devices, making it versatile for compatibility testing.

Cons:

  • Delayed iOS Support: Newer iOS versions, such as iOS 16, 17, and 18, have experienced significant delays in adoption, limiting testers ability to validate apps against the latest simulators.
  • Lack of Performant Infrastructure: Sauce Labs struggles with delivering high-performing virtual environments for Safari, Android, and iOS testing, leading to slower execution times and inefficiencies in mobile testing workflows.
  • Slow Innovation and Market Timing: While Sauce Labs is developing a more affordable alternative for visual testing, the pace of innovation has lagged behind competitors and new features may arrive too late to compete effectively in a rapidly evolving market.

Conclusion:

Sauce Labs remains a reliable platform with strong real-device stability, making it a good choice for scaling large test suites. However, the lack of a high-performance infrastructure for virtual Desktop Safari, Android, and iOS environments, combined with the slow adoption of new iOS versions, reduces its effectiveness for modern testing needs. While efforts to introduce new features, such as an affordable visual testing solution, are promising, the slower pace of innovation may hinder its competitiveness in the current market.
We recommend placing Sauce Labs on hold for new projects and exploring alternatives like BrowserStack or LambdaTest, which offer faster support, more modern infrastructure, and competitive features for mobile and web testing.

name: Appium
description:
Description:

Appium is a cross-platform automation tool for mobile applications, enabling black-box testing by interacting with apps as a user would, without requiring source code. It supports both Android and iOS, making it ideal for comprehensive mobile testing.

Pros:

  • Cross-platform: Appium supports Android and iOS for versatile automation.
  • Multi-language support: Appium is compatible with JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Ruby, and Python.
  • Black-box approach: Appium simulates real user interactions without requiring source code access.

Cons:

  • Lacks framework features: Appium requires additional setup for reporting and orchestration.
  • Learning curve: Appium can be challenging for teams new to automation due to its complexity and differing app interpretation and feature support across platforms.
  • Developer-focused tools preferred: Tools like Espresso or XCUITest may better suit developers than Appium in certain cases.

Conclusion:

Appium is a strong choice for QA teams requiring cross-platform, standards-based automation for black-box testing. Its ability to simulate real user interactions without requiring source code access makes it particularly valuable for testing diverse mobile applications. However, Appium may not be the best fit for developer-driven testing workflows, where tools like Espresso and XCUITest offer tighter platform integration.
Despite its learning curve, and the need for supplementary tools, Appium remains a top option for QA-focused automation, especially for teams seeking flexibility and cross-platform compatibility.

name: Cypress
description:
Description:

Until April 2024, Cypress was the most downloaded end-to-end (E2E) testing tool according to npmtrends.com, showcasing its popularity. It is a commercially maintained tool with excellent free resources to get started, including very clear and extensive documentation. Integrating Cypress into your developer workflow is easy; it has a user-friendly UI that can be opened next to the browser, making it easy to debug your code.
A great feature of Cypress is its ability to create component tests. Testing components in isolation is generally more stable and decreases the amount of E2E tests. Cypress is able to load-balance the tests and run them in parallel via the Cypress Cloud, available in the free tier. Cypress also has several other features that are great for scaling your test suite, such as extended reporting, test replay (because debugging in CI can be hard), UI coverage, and more.
Some of these features are only available in the paid version of Cypress, but the free tier of the Cypress Cloud is still very powerful. More on the pricing can be found here: Cypress Pricing.

Pros:

  • Beginner-friendly: Offers a smooth learning curve with extensive documentation, free training resources, and practical examples, making it easy to adopt.
  • Comprehensive free features: Provides everything needed for initial test automation without requiring payment.
  • Integrated debugging tools: Built-in debugging capabilities simplify troubleshooting during development.
  • Load-balanced parallelism: Cypress can load-balance your tests over multiple CI runners (paid feature).

Cons:

  • Expensive cloud features: Paid Cypress Cloud features like Flake Detection and Flaky Test Analytics are great, but because pricing is based on the number of tests run, costs can be very high for larger test suites.
  • Cypress Cloud data storage: Data storage for the Cypress Cloud is located in the US, which can be a problem for companies that are not allowed to store data outside of the EU.

Conclusion:

Cypress is a great choice for teams starting their automation journey or teams relying on more outdated test frameworks, offering robust free features and a beginner-friendly experience.

name: Playwright
description:
Description:

Playwright, released in January 2020, has quickly established itself as a strong alternative to tools like Cypress. It supports all major browser engines (Chromium, WebKit, and Firefox), operating systems, and programming languages. What sets Playwright apart are its automatic waiting features, actionability checks, and web-first assertions, making it a highly reliable tool for end-to-end (E2E) testing.
Playwright also supports testing on real, unmodified browsers, including Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge, though with some feature limitations.
While Playwright is primarily intended for E2E testing, Playwright can also easily be used for REST API testing

Pros:

  • Actionability checks: Playwright ensures elements are actionable (e.g., visible, attached to the DOM) before interactions, significantly reducing flaky tests.
  • Web-first assertions: Playwright automatically retries assertions until conditions are met, minimizing test failures due to timing issues.
  • Debugging and (API-)testing features: Includes powerful tools like network interception, mocking, and tracing, giving developers granular control over their tests.

Cons:

  • Proprietary protocol: Playwright uses a proprietary protocol for its enhanced features, relying on modified browser engines. While it supports real browsers like Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge, features such as network interception may not fully work with these unmodified browsers. Additionally, for Safari, Playwright relies on the WebKit engine rather than testing the real, unmodified Safari browser, which may lead to minor inconsistencies in Safari-specific environments.
  • Limited component testing control: While excellent for E2E testing, Playwright’s support for component testing is not as mature as some alternatives.
  • Open-source uncertainty: Although open to contributions, Playwright is developed and controlled by Microsoft, making its long-term future subject to corporate decisions.

Conclusion:

Playwright is an excellent contender for E2E-style tests with its automatic waiting capabilities, web-first assertions, and powerful debugging features like network interception, mocking, and tracing. Its ability to support both real browsers (e.g., Chrome and Edge) and modified engines provides flexibility for testing needs. However, its reliance on a proprietary protocol, including the use of WebKit for Safari-like testing, introduces some limitations when aiming for true browser fidelity. Additionally, while Playwright is open source, its development is controlled by Microsoft, meaning its long-term future is tied to corporate priorities.
Despite these trade-offs, Playwright's rich feature set, reliability, and active development make it a strong contender for modern cross-browser automation. We strongly recommend Playwright for teams looking for a versatile and robust component testing/E2E testing framework.

name: Puppeteer
description:
Description:

Puppeteer, developed by Google, is a browser automation tool popular for Chrome and Firefox automation. While it provides rich tracing capabilities and WebDriver BiDi support, its limited browser support and reliance on additional tools make it less ideal for comprehensive test automation.

Pros:

  • Rich tracing capabilities: Provides detailed browser tracing for debugging and performance analysis.
  • WebDriver BiDi support: Enables advanced browser interaction features.

Cons:

  • Limited browser support: Works primarily with Chrome for Testing and Firefox.
  • Requires additional tools: Needs integration with libraries like Mocha and Chai for full test automation functionality.

Conclusion:

Puppeteer is a powerful tool for browser automation but falls short for modern test automation due to its limited browser support and reliance on external libraries.
For new projects, tools like Playwright, which offer broader browser support and integrated features, are often a better choice.

name: rest-assured.net
description:
Description:

Rest-Assured.NET is a C#/.NET implementation inspired by the widely popular Rest-Assured library for testing REST APIs in Java. Developed by Bas Dijkstra, it offers a fluent and expressive syntax for testing RESTful APIs, making it a promising tool for .NET developers. While still relatively young, it has been under rapid development and is gaining attention as a welcome addition to the .NET ecosystem. Being free and open source, Rest-Assured.NET provides an accessible and cost-effective solution for API testing.

Pros:

  • Expressive syntax: Enables intuitive and human-readable API testing, reducing boilerplate code.
  • Rapid development: Continues to evolve quickly, adding features and improving functionality.
  • Open source: Free to use, with transparent contributions and an opportunity for developers to help shape its future.

Cons:

  • Relatively new: As a young tool, it may lack the maturity and extensive ecosystem of more established API testing frameworks.
  • Limited resources: Documentation and community support are still growing, which may pose challenges for teams adopting it.
  • Potential gaps in features: May not yet have all the capabilities found in more mature API testing tools.

Conclusion:

Rest-Assured.NET is an exciting newcomer for .NET developers seeking a fluent and expressive way to test REST APIs. Its free and open source nature makes it accessible, while its rapid development promises a growing feature set.
However, as a relatively new tool, it may lack the maturity and ecosystem support required for highly complex or large-scale API testing projects. We recommend assessing Rest-Assured.NET for smaller projects or teams looking for a modern, .NET-native API testing solution. For more established projects with advanced requirements, consider evaluating other mature tools while keeping an eye on the continued growth of this promising framework.
As an open source project, contributions are highly valued, and developers interested in strengthening the .NET API testing ecosystem are encouraged to support the project. If you want to help improve Rest-Assured.NET, consider reaching out to Bas Dijkstra and contributing to the community to make this a strong alternative for .NET API testing.

name: Robot Framework
description:
Description:

Robot Framework is an open-source automation framework widely used for its versatility and simplicity. It supports numerous libraries written in Python, Java, and other programming languages, making it adaptable to a variety of testing needs. Its human-readable, keyword-driven syntax is designed to simplify test creation and foster collaboration between technical and non-technical stakeholders.

Pros:

  • Human-friendly syntax: Robot Framework simplifies communication with stakeholders through readable and descriptive test cases.
  • Easy integration: Robot Framework integrates seamlessly with Python, Java, and other languages, enabling teams to customize and expand their capabilities to fit project requirements.
  • Active community: A large, supportive Robot Framework community provides extensive resources and assistance.

Cons:

  • Verbose test cases: The human-readable syntax in Robot Framework can lead to longer test scripts, which may be harder to maintain in large projects.
  • Learning curve for customization: Customizing Robot Framework libraries or creating complex tests may require strong programming skills.

Conclusion:

Robot Framework is an excellent choice for teams seeking a versatile and easy-to-use test automation solution, especially for projects where collaboration between technical and non-technical stakeholders is a priority. While its verbose syntax and customization requirements can pose challenges for large or highly technical projects, its extensibility and strong community support make it a powerful tool for test automation. For teams looking for flexibility, readability, and community-driven resources, Robot Framework is highly recommended.

name: Selenium
description:
Description:

Selenium, first released in 2004, is the foundation of modern web automation and the driving force behind the W3C WebDriver standard. Its widespread adoption has helped establish industry-wide best practices for cross-browser automation. With support for multiple programming languages and real browser automation, Selenium remains a key tool in the test automation ecosystem.

Pros:

  • W3C standards compliant: As the foundation of the WebDriver standard, Selenium ensures reliability and compatibility across modern browsers.
  • Cross-browser support: Works with all major browsers, making it a versatile solution for automation.
  • Upcoming WebDriver BiDi support: Promises advanced interaction capabilities.
  • Large community and ecosystem: Extensive resources, integrations, and an active contributor base provide strong long-term support.

Cons:

  • Additional tooling required: Requires integration with other tools for reporting, parallelization, and a full automation setup.
  • Less intuitive: Compared to newer tools like Playwright or Cypress, the setup and API can be more complex to learn.

Conclusion:

Selenium has been instrumental in shaping web automation standards, and its reliability and broad ecosystem continue to make it a viable choice for cross-browser testing. While it may not have the streamlined usability of newer tools, its adherence to industry standards and strong community support keep it relevant in many automation strategies.
We put Selenium on hold for starting new projects if you are looking for a more modern and streamlined experience, as using Selenium often requires building your own framework or evaluating various, and sometimes less maintained, community framework implementations.
However, if you are already using Selenium, we recommend evaluating BiDi support and considering it on your feature roadmap to assess how it could enhance your project in the future.

name: TestCafe
description:
Description:

TestCafe is a Node.js-based, open-source end-to-end (E2E) testing framework designed for modern web applications. It uses a proprietary protocol instead of relying on WebDriver or browser extensions, offering a simpler setup and execution process. While TestCafe provides cross-browser support and ease of use, its proprietary approach and lack of advanced features make it less competitive in today's automation landscape.

Pros:

  • Easy setup and usage: Doesn't require browser plugins or WebDriver, making it straightforward to install and run tests.
  • Cross-browser support: Works out of the box with modern browsers, simplifying compatibility testing.
  • Built-in parallel execution: Offers parallel test execution without requiring additional tools or configurations.

Cons:

  • Proprietary protocol: Relies on its own protocol instead of standardized tools like WebDriver, which may result in inconsistent browser behavior and limited flexibility.
  • Limited ecosystem and community support: Compared to tools like Playwright or WebdriverIO, its library and community are smaller, limiting available resources and extensions.
  • Performance issues in large test suites: Can struggle with large-scale or complex test suites, leading to slower execution times.

Conclusion:

The proprietary protocol and ease of use make TestCafe an appealing choice for teams with minimal requirements or simpler workflows. However, its lack of advanced features, smaller community, and scalability challenges place it at a disadvantage compared to modern alternatives.
We recommend putting TestCafe on hold for now and considering alternatives like Playwright or WebdriverIO, which offer broader capabilities, better performance, and adherence to industry standards.

name: WebdriverIO
description:
Description:

WebdriverIO is a robust, open-source automation framework based on W3C WebDriver standards, enabling reliable cross-platform testing for web and mobile applications. It supports emulators, simulators, real devices, and virtual machines, making it a versatile choice for comprehensive automation needs.

Pros:

  • Standards-based compatibility: Ensures reliability across browsers and devices.
  • Extensive plugins: Offers a rich set of plugins and services that enable seamless customization and CI/CD integration.
  • BiDi protocol support: Provides advanced, real-time testing capabilities through BiDi protocol support.

Cons:

  • Steeper learning curve: Can be harder to learn compared to tools like Cypress and Playwright.
  • Reliance on WebDriver: While WebDriver is standards-compliant, it may lag behind proprietary protocols like Playwright’s in terms of speed and advanced browser-specific features.

Conclusion:

WebdriverIO is ideal for teams seeking a standards-compliant, future-proof framework with robust community support and advanced features. With the addition of BiDi, the WebDriver protocol now includes the observability features you may be accustomed to in other tools like Playwright.
We hope and expect that this puts WebDriver and thus WebdriverIO back on the map!
